mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Bosnia and Herzegovina on?
mapQuestions.answer: Europe, in the western Balkans. Bosnia and Herzegovina (often shortened to BiH) is sandwiched between Croatia, Serbia and Montenegro 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Bosnia and Herzegovina?
mapQuestions.answer: Sarajevo, sitting in a deep mountain valley. Around 275,000 people. Famous for being where Eastern, Western, and Ottoman cultures all meet within a few cobblestone streets.

mapQuestions.question: What language do people speak there?
mapQuestions.answer: Bosnian, Croatian and Serbian, all three are official. They are very similar (you can usually understand all three with one) and written in both Latin and Cyrillic alphabets.

mapQuestions.question: Why are there mosques and churches almost next to each other?
mapQuestions.answer: Bosnia and Herzegovina is one of the most religiously mixed places in Europe. Bosnian Muslims, Orthodox Christians and Catholic Christians have lived here side by side for centuries. In central Sarajevo you can hear a mosque's call to prayer and a church's bells from the same street corner.

mapQuestions.question: How mountainous is Bosnia and Herzegovina?
mapQuestions.answer: Very. About 50% of the country is forested mountain. The Dinaric Alps run down the country, with peaks over 2,300 m. Sarajevo hosted the Winter Olympics in 1984.

foods.name: Ćevapi
foods.description: Small grilled minced-beef fingers, served in a soft warm flatbread ("somun") with chopped raw onion and a dollop of sour-cream "kajmak". The national meal. Every town claims the best ćevapi.

foods.name: Burek
foods.description: Flaky filo-pastry pie filled with meat (just "burek"), cheese ("sirnica"), spinach ("zeljanica") or potato ("krompiruša"), eaten with yogurt for breakfast.

foods.name: Bosanski lonac
foods.description: "Bosnian pot": layered chunks of meat, cabbage, carrots, potatoes and parsnips slow-cooked for hours in a tall clay pot. Traditional family Sunday dish.

foods.name: Pljeskavica
foods.description: A big, flat Balkan burger patty of mixed minced meat, grilled over coals and stuffed inside flatbread with salad and ajvar (sweet pepper relish).

foods.name: Baklava
foods.description: Layers of thin filo pastry with walnuts or pistachios between, soaked in sweet syrup. Ottoman heritage. Eaten in tiny squares with strong Bosnian coffee.

foods.name: Bosanska kafa
foods.description: Bosnian coffee: thick unfiltered coffee made by simmering grounds in a small copper pot ("džezva"), served with a sugar cube and Turkish delight on the side. Slow-drink it.

scavengerItems.label: The Old Bridge (Stari Most) of Mostar arching over the green Neretva river
A small copper "džezva" pot brewing strong Bosnian coffee
An Ottoman-era mosque dome and minaret in Sarajevo or Mostar
An Orthodox church and a Catholic church within walking distance of each other
The Sebilj wooden fountain in Sarajevo's Old Town
Snow-covered Dinaric Alps from a mountain road
Bright blue-green water of the Una river or Kravice waterfalls
The yellow Sarajevo tram, still rattling along the same line for 130 years
Stalls of metalwork, jewellery, rugs and copper coffee sets in Baščaršija bazaar
The "Šargan Eight" old narrow-gauge railway running into the mountains
Wild bear tracks (or a bear, if very lucky) in Sutjeska National Park
The "Tunnel of Hope" museum at Sarajevo Airport, a powerful site from the 1990s war

geoMatches.description: The capital, in a deep valley ringed by mountains. East meets West in a few streets: Ottoman mosques and bazaars, Habsburg cafés, Communist-era blocks, modern Sarajevo all overlap.
A small Herzegovinian city famous for its 16th-century Ottoman Old Bridge ("Stari Most"), destroyed in the 1990s war and painstakingly rebuilt by 2004. Now a UNESCO World Heritage site.
The biggest city in the Bosnian Serb-majority part of the country. Riverside cafés along the Vrbas, an Ottoman fortress (Kastel), and Orthodox cathedrals.
A wild, mountainous national park on the Montenegrin border, with primeval Perućica forest (one of the last in Europe), Maglić peak (2,386 m), and bears, wolves and chamois.
A spectacular semi-circle of waterfalls dropping 25 m into a green natural swimming pool near Mostar. Bosnia's answer to Plitvice.
A medieval town with a 21-metre waterfall right in the middle of it, a hilltop fortress, and the underground catacombs of a 15th-century Bosnian queen.

senses.smell: Smoke from a ćevapi grill, fresh-ground Bosnian coffee in a copper džezva, sweet syrup soaking into baklava, fresh bread from a Sarajevo bakery at 5am, pine forests in Sutjeska…
senses.hear: "Selam!" and "Dobar dan!" alongside each other, the call to prayer overlapping church bells in central Sarajevo, the yellow Sarajevo tram bell, the rush of the Neretva river under Mostar's bridge, divers shouting before they jump…
senses.taste: Smoky grilled ćevapi with chopped raw onion, sweet syrupy baklava, thick rich Bosnian coffee, sharp ajvar pepper relish, soft warm flatbread torn straight from the oven…
senses.feel: Cobblestones of Baščaršija bazaar underfoot, the cold spray of Kravice falls, the steam of strong Bosnian coffee on your face, the warmth of a wood-fired Sarajevo café in winter, the smooth carved stone of Mostar's bridge…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Why is the Old Bridge of Mostar so important?
stories.body: The Old Bridge of Mostar ("Stari Most") is an Ottoman stone arch from 1566, built by an Ottoman architect called Mimar Hayruddin. It is one of the most beautiful stone bridges in the world: a single perfect arch spanning the green Neretva river. For over 400 years, locals jumped off it for fun (a 24 m drop) and traders walked across it between the Christian and Muslim halves of the town. In the 1990s war the bridge was tragically destroyed. After the war, the international community rebuilt it stone by stone using original techniques, finished in 2004. Today young men still leap into the river (now sometimes for tourist donations), and the bridge stands again as a symbol of a city that refused to stay broken. UNESCO put it on the World Heritage list in 2005.

stories.question: Why is Sarajevo called "the Jerusalem of Europe"?
stories.body: Within about 400 metres of each other in central Sarajevo you can stand outside a Sunni mosque, an Orthodox cathedral, a Catholic cathedral, and a Sephardic synagogue. Bosnian Muslims, Orthodox Christians, Catholic Christians and a small Jewish community have lived in Sarajevo for centuries, sharing markets, schools and neighbourhoods. The mix gave the city the nickname "Jerusalem of Europe". You walk through the Ottoman Baščaršija bazaar, cross an invisible line, and you are suddenly in a Habsburg quarter of cafés and grand stone buildings. The city has been a meeting point of east and west, faiths and empires, for hundreds of years.

stories.question: Why is Bosnian coffee so different?
stories.body: Bosnian coffee is brewed completely differently from espresso or American filter coffee. Ground beans go into a small copper pot called a "džezva". Hot water is added. The pot is heated slowly over a stove until it foams up. The first foam is poured into a tiny cup, then the rest of the coffee is gently poured on top. You drink it WITHOUT stirring (the grounds settle at the bottom), with a sugar cube on the side, often dipping a Turkish-delight cube into the coffee between sips. The ritual takes 30 minutes minimum. Hosts make Bosnian coffee for every guest the moment they walk in. Bosnian coffee is more than a drink, it is a way of slowing down.
stories.thinkingQuestion: A 30-minute coffee ritual is built into everyday life here. What slow ritual would you want to keep in your own life?

stories.question: How did Sarajevo start World War I?
stories.body: On 28 June 1914, a young Bosnian Serb activist called Gavrilo Princip stood on a Sarajevo street corner and shot Archduke Franz Ferdinand, the heir to the Austro-Hungarian throne, and his wife Sophie. Within five weeks, that single moment set off a chain reaction of alliance treaties that pulled Russia, Germany, France, Britain and eventually most of the world into World War One. Around 20 million people died in the four years of war that followed. Today the exact street corner where the shooting happened is marked with a small plaque and a museum. Walking past it is a sobering reminder of how a single moment in one small city can change everything.

stories.question: What happened in the Bosnian War?
stories.body: When Yugoslavia broke apart in the early 1990s, Bosnia and Herzegovina declared independence in 1992. What followed was the most painful war Europe has seen since 1945. Sarajevo was besieged for nearly four years (the longest siege of a capital city in modern history). Mostar's Old Bridge was destroyed. Around 100,000 people died across all sides, and millions were displaced. The Dayton Peace Agreement in 1995 ended the war and divided the country into two political "entities" that still exist today: the Federation of Bosnia and Herzegovina, and Republika Srpska. The country is now at peace, but the memory is real and very present. Bosnians have rebuilt with care and patience, and visitors are welcomed warmly. Visiting respectfully, listening to people's stories, and seeing what has been rebuilt is part of what makes Bosnia such a meaningful place to visit.
stories.thinkingQuestion: A country can suffer something this hard and still welcome visitors warmly afterwards. What does that say about people?

convoQuestions.question: Best food in Bosnia so far?
Sarajevo, Mostar, mountains, waterfalls, which has felt the most "Bosnia"?
Did you sit through a 30-minute Bosnian coffee? How did it feel to slow down that much?
How does Sarajevo feel different from any other European capital you have been to?
The Old Bridge of Mostar was destroyed and rebuilt stone by stone. What does an act like that mean for a country?

wordSearch.hint: Bosnian foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Brown bear
animals.description: Sutjeska National Park has one of the last small populations of European brown bear. Around 500 live in Bosnia and Herzegovina overall.

animals.name: Grey wolf
animals.description: Bosnia's deep forests still host a real wolf population in the high mountains and Sutjeska area.

animals.name: Red deer
animals.description: Common in the spruce and beech forests of central Bosnia. Listen for the autumn roaring of the bucks.

animals.name: Bosnian mountain horse
animals.description: A small sturdy local breed used by upland villages for working steep ground. Pony-sized but very strong.

animals.name: Golden eagle
animals.description: Nests in the Dinaric Alps. A few dozen pairs across the country.

animals.name: Eurasian lynx
animals.description: Rare but real. A small lynx population lives in the deepest forests of Bosnia, mostly in the south-east.

animals.name: Wild Bosnian horse herd of Livno
animals.description: A herd of around 500 semi-feral horses roams the high mountain plateau near Livno. Famous as a photography subject.
